text:
GENITOURINARY ONCOLOGY FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Department of Medical Oncology -- Genitourinary Oncology Program

ATTENDING: [Attending Oncologist]
FELLOW: [Fellow]
PATIENT: Gerald Hutchinson
DOB: [on file]
MRN: [on file]

---

REASON FOR VISIT / CHIEF COMPLAINT

Mr. Hutchinson is a 68-year-old male with high-risk locally advanced prostate adenocarcinoma (cT3bN1M0, Gleason 4+5=9, Grade Group 5, PSA at diagnosis 87.4 ng/mL) presenting today for his 12-month follow-up visit following initiation of androgen deprivation therapy with leup acetate combined with Zytiga acetate plus prednisone, and completion of definitive pelvic intensity-modulated radiation therapy. This is his first visit to our GU oncology program, having been referred by his urologist following completion of his radiation course. He is accompanied by his wife.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Hutchinson was diagnosed approximately 12 months ago with high-risk locally advanced prostate cancer on the basis of an elevated PSA of 87.4 ng/mL, a 12-core transrectal ultrasound-guided prostate biopsy demonstrating Gleason 4+5=9 (Grade Group 5) adenocarcinoma in 10 of 12 cores (maximum core involvement 95%), and multiparametric MRI confirming bilateral tumor with extracapsular extension, bilateral seminal vesicle invasion, and a 1.8 cm right obturator lymph node along with two smaller right iliac chain nodes suspicious for nodal metastases (cT3bN1M0, AJCC Stage IVA). Conventional staging with whole-body bone scan and CT chest/abdomen/pelvis showed no evidence of distant osseous or visceral metastases at that time.

He was started on androgen deprivation therapy with leup acetate 22.5 mg IM every 3 months, along with abi acetate (Zytiga) 1000 mg orally daily and prednisone 5 mg twice daily, consistent with intensified hormonal therapy for high-risk disease. He achieved castrate testosterone levels within approximately 4 weeks (documented at 14 ng/dL). Approximately 3 months after initiating systemic therapy, he completed a course of definitive IMRT/IGRT to 78 Gy in 39 fractions to the prostate and seminal vesicles and 54 Gy in 39 fractions to the pelvic nodal region. He tolerated radiation well overall, with grade 1-2 urinary frequency and urgency, and mild rectal irritation that has largely resolved.

His PSA nadir on combined therapy was 0.4 ng/mL at approximately 6 months, with his most recent PSA today measured at 0.2 ng/mL (further decline from the 6-month nadir). Testosterone continues to confirm castrate suppression at 11 ng/dL.

Currently, Mr. Hutchinson reports persistent grade 1 fatigue, which he describes as noticeable but not functionally limiting -- he continues to perform light daily activities, manages his household finances, and recently took a short vacation with his wife. He reports mild-to-moderate hot flashes several times per day, which he finds bothersome but manageable. He notes occasional nocturia (1-2 times per night), which he believes is residual from radiation-related urethral irritation rather than worsening obstruction. He denies any bone pain, hematuria, rectal bleeding, dysuria, or lower extremity edema. He has not had any falls. Appetite is intact. Weight is stable.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY

1. Prostate adenocarcinoma -- high-risk locally advanced (cT3bN1M0, Gleason 9, PSA 87.4 ng/mL) -- as above
2. Hypertension -- well-controlled on lisinopril and amlodipine
3. Type 2 diabetes mellitus -- managed with metformin; last HbA1c 7.1% (per PCP records)
4. Hyperlipidemia -- on atorvastatin
5. Former smoker -- 20 pack-year history, quit at age 55; no current COPD or significant pulmonary disease
6. Benign prostatic hyperplasia -- preexisting, contributed to baseline nocturia prior to diagnosis
7. Osteopenia -- noted on baseline DXA prior to ADT initiation (T-score lumbar spine -1.3); on supplemental calcium and vitamin D

SURGICAL HISTORY
- Appendectomy (remote, childhood)
- No prior prostate surgery

---

FAMILY HISTORY

Significant for prostate cancer: father diagnosed at age 65; paternal uncle deceased from prostate cancer. No known family history of breast, ovarian, colorectal, pancreatic, or other BRCA-related malignancies reported by patient, though detailed multigenerational history is limited. Given the strong paternal lineage prostate cancer history and his own high-grade disease at relatively early age (67), germline testing for hereditary cancer syndromes has been discussed with the patient and will be deferred until a future visit when we discuss long-term risk management.

---

SOCIAL HISTORY

Retired civil engineer; lives with his wife of 42 years in a single-story home. Functionally independent. Drives independently. Former smoker (20 pack-years, cessation at age 55); no current tobacco use. Rare alcohol use (1-2 drinks per week). No illicit drug use. Good social support system. Active in his church community. Originally from Minnesota; Northern European descent.

---

ALLERGIES

NKDA (No Known Drug Allergies)

---

MEDICATIONS

1. leup acetate (Lupron Depot) 22.5 mg IM every 3 months (next injection due in approximately 6 weeks)
2. abi acetate (Zytiga) 1000 mg orally once daily on empty stomach
3. Prednisone 5 mg orally twice daily (with Zytiga)
4. Lisinopril 10 mg orally daily
5. Amlodipine 5 mg orally daily
6. Metformin 1000 mg orally twice daily
7. Atorvastatin 40 mg orally at bedtime
8. Calcium carbonate 500 mg orally twice daily
9. Vitamin D3 2000 IU orally daily
10. Aspirin 81 mg orally daily

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Positive for: fatigue (grade 1, chronic), hot flashes (multiple per day, bothersome), nocturia (1-2x/night, stable-to-improving), mild mood changes (periodic irritability, attributed to ADT).
Negative for: bone pain, headache, visual changes, dyspnea, chest pain, palpitations, hematuria, rectal bleeding, lower extremity swelling, paresthesias, cognitive dysfunction, falls, weight loss, significant anorexia.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vitals: Blood pressure 128/76 mmHg, Heart rate 68 bpm, Temperature 36.8 C, Respiratory rate 16, Weight 184 lbs (BMI 26.2), Oxygen saturation 97% on room air.

General: Well-appearing male in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3. Conversational and engaged. Appears stated age.
Head/Neck: Normocephalic, atraumatic. No cervical, supraclavicular, or submandibular lymphadenopathy palpable.
Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No lower extremity pitting edema.
Pulmonary: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes or crackles.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatosplenomegaly. Bowel sounds present.
Musculoskeletal: No tenderness to palpation of the spine. No costovertebral angle tenderness. Full range of motion in bilateral lower extremities. No joint swelling.
Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Strength 5/5 in upper and lower extremities bilaterally. No focal neurological deficits.
Genitourinary/Rectal (DRE): Prostate without palpable residual nodularity. No masses or induration appreciated. Rectal mucosa intact. No anorectal tenderness (residual radiation proctitis has clinically resolved).
Skin: No rash, jaundice, or concerning skin lesions.
ECOG Performance Status: 1 (symptomatic but fully ambulatory, capable of all self-care, up and about >50% of waking hours).

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

Today's values:
- PSA: 0.2 ng/mL (continued decline; PSA nadir achieved)
- Testosterone: 11 ng/dL (confirmed castrate suppression)
- CBC: WBC 5.4 x10^9/L, Hemoglobin 12.6 g/dL, Hematocrit 38.1%, Platelets 218 x10^9/L (mild normocytic anemia, likely multifactorial: ADT effect, chronic disease)
- CMP: Sodium 138 mEq/L, Potassium 4.1 mEq/L, Creatinine 1.0 mg/dL (eGFR ~72 mL/min), BUN 18 mg/dL, Glucose 112 mg/dL, ALT 28 U/L, AST 24 U/L, Alkaline phosphatase 82 U/L, Total bilirubin 0.7 mg/dL, Albumin 4.0 g/dL
- HbA1c: 7.3% (slightly elevated from prior; discussed with patient and PCP to follow)
- Lipid panel: LDL 88 mg/dL (on statin therapy)
- Potassium and liver enzymes: within acceptable limits on abi/prednisone

Prior PSA trend:
- At diagnosis (12 months ago): 87.4 ng/mL
- 6 months after therapy initiation: 0.4 ng/mL
- Today (12 months): 0.2 ng/mL

---

IMAGING RESULTS

No new imaging performed at today's visit. Per review of prior records:
- Staging MRI pelvis and whole-body bone scan/CT C/A/P at diagnosis confirmed cT3bN1M0 disease.
- Surveillance CT C/A/P and bone scan at approximately 24 months is planned per protocol. Given today's excellent biochemical response, no urgent imaging is indicated.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

1. HIGH-RISK LOCALLY ADVANCED PROSTATE ADENOCARCINOMA (cT3bN1M0, Gleason 4+5=9, Grade Group 5) -- EXCELLENT BIOCHEMICAL RESPONSE TO MULTIMODAL THERAPY

Mr. Hutchinson has achieved an outstanding PSA response to combined intensified ADT (leup + Zytiga/prednisone) and definitive pelvic IMRT. His PSA has fallen from a diagnostic level of 87.4 ng/mL to a current nadir of 0.2 ng/mL -- a >99.7% decline. Testosterone remains deeply suppressed at 11 ng/dL. This degree of PSA response at 12 months is associated with favorable long-term outcomes in the setting of high-risk disease treated with combined-modality intensification.

At this time, there is no clinical, biochemical, or radiographic evidence of active cancer. We will continue his current systemic therapy regimen unchanged.

Plan:
- Continue Lupron acetate 22.5 mg IM every 3 months (next due in approximately 6 weeks; to be administered by his urologist as arranged).
- Continue abi acetate 1000 mg orally daily on empty stomach with prednisone 5 mg BID.
- Surveillance labs (PSA, testosterone, CBC, CMP, LFTs) every 3 months.
- Surveillance imaging (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and bone scan) planned at approximately 24 months from initiation of therapy (i.e., approximately 12 months from today) given stable biochemical response.
- Counsel patient on symptoms of disease recurrence: new bone pain, constitutional symptoms, lymphedema.

2. ADT-RELATED ADVERSE EFFECTS -- FATIGUE, HOT FLASHES, NOCTURIA

Fatigue (grade 1) is expected and well-documented with ADT plus abi. Mild anemia (Hgb 12.6 g/dL) may be a contributing factor. Patient is not exercise-limited; regular low-intensity aerobic exercise encouraged (walking, cycling) to mitigate fatigue and preserve lean body mass. Hot flashes multiple times per day -- patient declines pharmacologic management at this time; counseled on venlafaxine and medroxyprogesterone as options if symptoms become more disruptive. Nocturia appears stable to improving, attributed to resolving radiation-related urethral inflammation.

3. BONE HEALTH ON ADT

Baseline osteopenia documented (lumbar T-score -1.3) prior to initiating ADT. Patient already on calcium 500 mg BID and vitamin D3 2000 IU daily. Continuing current supplementation. DXA to be repeated at approximately 24 months on ADT. Zoledronic acid or denosumab not initiated at this time given osteopenic (not osteoporotic) baseline and relatively short-duration ADT thus far; will reassess with repeat DXA.

4. DIABETES MANAGEMENT

HbA1c mildly elevated at 7.3% (up from 7.1% per recent PCP note). ADT is known to worsen glycemic control and insulin sensitivity. Coordination with primary care provider placed; metformin dose optimization may be warranted. Patient counseled to monitor blood glucose more closely.

5. GERMLINE TESTING CONSIDERATIONS

Given patient's strong family history of prostate cancer (father and paternal uncle), his own high-grade/high-volume disease at age 67, and emerging data on germline BRCA2/ATM/BRCA1 mutations in hereditary prostate cancer, referral to genetic counseling will be arranged for consideration of hereditary cancer panel testing. This will be formalized at next visit if patient agrees.

6. FOLLOW-UP

Return to GU oncology clinic in 3 months for PSA check, testosterone level, and symptom assessment. Earlier return if new bone pain, lower extremity weakness, or urinary/bowel changes develop.

Time spent with patient and family in counseling and care coordination: approximately 45 minutes.

Electronically signed by: [Attending Oncologist], MD
Genitourinary Medical Oncology
[Institution Name]
